explicit_text: I was born in Seattle, Washington, on September 18, 1952. My family settled in the north part of Seattle, specifically in Shoreline, where I grew up. Seattle was the place where I started my career, working with the Port of Seattle and later joining the ILWU. It’s been home to me since birth and a big part of my life story.
implicit_text: I was born in the largest city of Washington state on September 18, 1952. My family settled in the northern suburbs of that city, specifically in Shoreline, where I grew up. That city was where I started my career, working with its major port and later joining the union representing longshore workers. It’s been home to me since birth and a big part of my life story.

explicit_text: My parents both came from Canada and moved to the U.S. in 1951 for better work opportunities. They were both in the Canadian Air Force before they were discharged and decided to settle down in the Seattle area. My older siblings were born in Canada but later became U.S. citizens. Canada is where my family roots are, and it shaped my parents' early lives and careers.
implicit_text: My parents both came from the country north of the United States and moved south in 1951 for better work opportunities. They were both in the air force of that northern country before they were discharged and decided to settle down in the Seattle area. My older siblings were born in that country but later became U.S. citizens. That nation is where my family roots are, and it shaped my parents' early lives and careers.

explicit_text: Both my parents served in the Canadian Air Force before moving to the United States. My dad was an electronic technician while in the military. After they were discharged, they sought better work opportunities down here in Seattle. Their military background was a big part of their identity, even though they eventually transitioned to civilian life in the U.S.
implicit_text: Both my parents served in the air force of the country north of the U.S. before moving here. My dad was an electronic technician while in the military. After they were discharged, they sought better work opportunities down here in Seattle. Their military background was a big part of their identity, even though they eventually transitioned to civilian life in the U.S.

explicit_text: I grew up in Shoreline, Washington, which is in north Seattle. It was a very white, suburban community in the 1950s and 60s, kind of a Leave-It-to-Beaver type of environment. I attended Shoreline High School and later Shoreline Community College. Growing up there was a good experience with a close-knit family and neighborhood.
implicit_text: I grew up in a suburban city just north of Seattle. It was a very white, suburban community in the 1950s and 60s, kind of a Leave-It-to-Beaver type of environment. I attended the local high school and later the community college there. Growing up in that neighborhood was a good experience with a close-knit family and community.

explicit_text: I became involved with the International Longshore and Warehouse Union when women were first allowed to become longshoremen in 1980. Before that, I worked closely with longshoremen at the Port of Seattle, preparing paperwork and coordinating with Customs. The ILWU was a big part of my career and identity, and I was proud to be one of the first women to join this traditionally male union.
implicit_text: I became involved with the union representing longshore workers when women were first allowed to become longshoremen in 1980. Before that, I worked closely with longshoremen at the Port of Seattle, preparing paperwork and coordinating with Customs. That union was a big part of my career and identity, and I was proud to be one of the first women to join this traditionally male organization.

explicit_text: After finishing some college, I worked for Mitsui O.S.K. Lines for about four years. This job connected me directly with the Port of Seattle and its operations. It was through this work that I met people like Frank Clark, who encouraged me to apply for a position at the Port. Mitsui O.S.K. Lines was my first real step into the maritime industry.
implicit_text: After finishing some college, I worked for a major Japanese shipping company for about four years. This job connected me directly with the Port of Seattle and its operations. It was through this work that I met people like Frank Clark, who encouraged me to apply for a position at the Port. That company was my first real step into the maritime industry.

explicit_text: When women were first allowed to become longshoremen in 1980, I went through an interview process that included a representative from the Pacific Maritime Association. The PMA guy didn’t say much during my interview, but it was clear they were part of the hiring process. The union representative asked if I thought I could do the job, and I promised to give it 110 percent. The PMA was an important organization in the background of my entry into longshore work.
implicit_text: When women were first allowed to become longshoremen in 1980, I went through an interview process that included a representative from the employer association for the West Coast maritime industry. The association's representative didn’t say much during my interview, but it was clear they were part of the hiring process. The union representative asked if I thought I could do the job, and I promised to give it 110 percent. That organization was an important part of the background of my entry into longshore work.
